seo.title: Blackholed Addresses on {{explorerName}}
seo.description: What blackholed addresses are on XRP and Xahau Ledgers, why they matter, and how accounts become blackholed.

paragraphs.intro: Blackholed addresses are {{explorerName}} wallet addresses from which funds can never be retrieved or spent. These addresses are permanently unmanageable, making it impossible to sign transactions from them. As a result, any {{nativeCurrency}} held in these addresses is permanently removed from circulation and no more tokens can be issued from them. Blackholed addresses can be created intentionally for specific purposes or occur unintentionally due to various reasons.
paragraphs.reason: One primary reason for blackholing an account is to prevent misuse or unauthorized access. This is particularly important for token issuers who want to ensure that no one can modify token settings or freeze assets after issuance. By blackholing the issuing account, they demonstrate that the token supply is immutable and free from central control.
paragraphs.how: An account becomes blackholed by disabling the master key and setting its regular key to a special publicly known blackholed address like rrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrBZbvji. If no active signer list is assigned, the account is completely inaccessible.
paragraphs.conclusion: Blackholed addresses serve an important role in the {{explorerName}} ecosystem by enhancing security, enforcing immutability, and affecting the overall token supply. By permanently locking accounts, projects can ensure decentralization and prevent unauthorized modifications. While blackholing is a useful tool, it should be done with careful consideration, as the process is irreversible.

exampleIntro: Let's view one example of a blackholed account and how it is highlighted on our website. As you can see, we mention that:

exampleBullets.regularKey: its regular key is set to one of the special publicly known addresses on {{explorerName}}.

specialAddresses.title: Special Addresses on {{explorerName}}
specialAddresses.intro: {{explorerName}} includes several publicly known blackholed addresses:
specialAddresses.name: Name

meanings.accountZero: The base58 encoding of the value 0 in the {{explorerName}}. Used by {{daemon}} as the issuer for {{nativeCurrency}} in peer-to-peer communications.
meanings.addressOne: The base58 encoding of the value 1 in the {{explorerName}}. Used as a placeholder for the issuer of a trustline balance in RippleState entries.
meanings.rippleName: Previously used by Ripple to reserve Ripple Names by requiring users to send {{nativeCurrency}} to this account.
meanings.nanAddress: Generated by older versions of ripple-lib when encoding NaN using the {{explorerName}}'s base58 string encoding format.
